questions.question: Describe the role of the Memory Address Register (MAR) and the Memory Data Register (MDR) in the process of fetching a word from memory.
keyPoints: The Memory Address Register (MAR) holds the address of the memory location to be accessed.
The Memory Data Register (MDR) holds the data word that is either read from or written to that memory location.
During a fetch, the processor places the desired address into the MAR, which is then sent to the memory unit.
The memory unit uses the address to retrieve the data, which is then placed onto the data bus and stored in the MDR for the processor to use.

questions.question: Explain why a memory read operation is considered a destructive read and what implication this has for the fetched data.
keyPoints: In many memory technologies, like DRAM, reading a memory cell discharges the capacitor that stores the data bit, destroying the original value.
Because the read is destructive, the data must be written back to the same memory location after it is read to preserve it.
This restore operation is often handled automatically by the memory controller or the DRAM interface logic, making the destructive nature transparent to the programmer.

questions.question: List the primary steps a processor must take to initiate a fetch of a single word from a memory address.
keyPoints: Place the address of the word onto the address bus (typically by loading it into the MAR).
Activate the read control signal (e.g., set MemRead to '1').
Wait for the memory to retrieve the data and place it on the data bus.
Capture the data from the data bus into a processor register (typically the MDR).
